cues.label: The lifecycle map
cues.headline: Raw text to a running model is seven contracts
cues.narration: The lesson opens with the map: getting from raw text to a running language model is seven distinct stages, each a contract between what came before and what comes next.

cues.label: The seven stages
cues.headline: Data → Tokenizer → Architecture → Training → Checkpoint → Quantization → Release
cues.narration: Each of the seven stages transforms what you have into what the next stage needs: clean data, a tokenizer, an initialized architecture, the training loop, checkpoints with evals, quantization, then packaging and release. Skip or break one and everything downstream breaks.

cues.label: Tokenization = translation
cues.headline: The tokenizer/model contract: swap it and the output turns to garbage
cues.narration: Tokenization is the translation layer between human text and model math. The tokenizer and model share a fixed-vocabulary contract: an ID means one learned vector. Swap the tokenizer at inference and the model silently produces nonsense with no error.

cues.label: Vocabulary size
cues.headline: 70 vs 32k vs 100k vs 262,144: vocab size sets the embedding table
cues.narration: Vocabulary size is a real design lever: a character tokenizer has about 70 entries, LLaMA about 32,000, GPT-4 about 100,000, and Gemma 4 uses 262,144. Bigger vocab means fewer tokens per sentence but a larger embedding table (over a billion parameters at 4,096 dims).

cues.label: Build a char tokenizer
cues.headline: Collect, sort, index: encode and decode — POTTERS
cues.narration: You build a character-level tokenizer the nanoGPT way: collect the unique characters, sort them, assign each an index; encoding maps char to index, decoding maps back. Remember the lifecycle with POTTERS: Plan, Organize, Tokenize, Train, Evaluate, Reduce, Serve.
